comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the impressive but imperfect humanoid robot tennis skills demonstrated in the video. Commenters express fascination with the technology's current limitations, potential future applications, and challenges in robotics state estimation and control.

comments.author: blueblisters
comments.text:
Very impressive. But it doesn’t solve the whole problem yet.

The robot and ball pose is estimated by high speed mocap cameras, and is fed to the policy.

I imagine estimating that with onboard cameras - how humans do it - is much harder.

Almost all of closed loop robotics is a state estimation problem. Control is “solved” if you can estimate state well enough.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights a key technical limitation in the demonstration: reliance on external motion capture for state estimation, which is a known challenge in robotics.

comments.author: ilaksh
comments.text:
We have just started ramping up practical use of imitation learning from human demonstrations in humanoids. A bigger development is that one or two projects are working on training foundational vision action language models based on large video datasets.

I think before the end of summer general purpose physical knowledge and capabilities will start to be demonstrated by one or more humanoid AI or robotics groups.

Maybe 18 months at the absolute latest.

I'm guessing by next year or 2028 there will be services where you can order a robot to come cook and or clean for you. By 2029 it should be quite affordable to get a humanoid on a short term rental.

Do we have any standard benchmarks for humanoids to do domestic tasks?
comments.analysis: This comment provides a knowledgeable forecast on the progress and future of humanoid robotics and AI, grounded in current research trends.

comments.author: ohyoutravel
comments.text: Why can some Temu humanoid robot do this sort of impressive, coordinated, high-speed thing, but Tesla Optimus completely sucks at everything unless they’re moving at 0.02m/s (and even then they’re not great)? Like, train this thing on the latent space of folding my clothes out of the dryer and I will send you my money.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid comparison between different humanoid robots, reflecting known performance differences, though somewhat anecdotal.

comments.author: V__
comments.text:
This is so interesting. Especially since it's kinda weird to train a robot to mimicking human play. I wonder what a perfect robot what actually behave like.

It wouldn't need to split-step to activate muscles, the footwork would probably be minimal. I imagine a lot of different unusual looking swings to confuse human players, while still making perfect contact. It could make really late drop shots or even rotate the racket at the last moment for crazy angles.

Would love to watch this.
comments.analysis: Offers an insightful perspective on how robots might surpass human play styles, though speculative.

comments.author: ordu
comments.text:
It is interesting to watch. The movements of the robot are robot-like. I mean, wtf, there were no robot playing tennis before, but I have an idea how a robot playing tennis would be like, and this video confirms my expectations. Sharp, unsure movements, a lot of hesitation, ...

Movies pictured robots like this long before this become possible, but how did producers guessed it?

Or maybe movies rendered different kinds of robots, but this video bring into my memory only those, that look like this. A kind of confirmation bias?
comments.analysis: Expresses subjective impressions and a cultural observation, but contains speculative elements about movies predicting robot behavior.

comments.author: hbcondo714
comments.text: Impressive! Looks like a nice alternative or evolutionary step for a ball machine. Either way, teach it to serve :)
comments.analysis: A positive but brief comment that suggests a practical next step, though lacking technical depth.

comments.author: Aboutplants
comments.text: Really impressive. In a few years there will be robotic AI instructors for the wealthy and their kids
comments.analysis: Makes a plausible prediction about future commercialization but is speculative and lacks supporting detail.

comments.author: KolmogorovComp
comments.text: Nothing constructive to say, besides that the video really shows we're entering into a Sci-fi era.
comments.analysis: Expresses excitement but does not contribute substantive analysis or information.

comments.author: Void_
comments.text: This just makes me want to play tennis right now. Such an addictive sports.
comments.analysis: A personal emotional reaction unrelated to the technical discussion.
